hook: KERB's food hall inside a Victorian cucumber-and-banana warehouse
facts: Opened by street-food collective KERB in 2019 in Thomas Neal's Warehouse
The building stored cucumbers and bananas for the old Covent Garden fruit-and-veg market
Around 20 traders over two floors, open seven days a week
Home to the cult Pick & Cheese conveyor-belt cheese bar
w: Bananas and cucumbers once ripened in this brick warehouse, freight for the old Covent Garden veg market a few streets over. KERB took it on in 2019 and packed twenty traders across two floors, plus a conveyor belt that ferries cheese past your stool. Fruit store turned feed trough.

hook: Sprawling Italian-led market in a former paper warehouse, final summer at Newington Causeway
facts: Opened 2016 on Newington Causeway in a disused paper-storage warehouse
The brand began at Milan's Expo 2015 before crossing to London
Site approved for demolition in 2026 for the Borough Triangle towers
Continues with World Cup screenings, kilo sales and community events into its last season
w: Born at Milan's 2015 Expo, this rambling Italian market landed in a derelict Elephant paper warehouse in 2016 and never tidied up the edges. Now living its last summer before 38-storey towers take the plot: World Cup screenings, kilo sales, beer in the yard. Going out loud, not quietly.

hook: Shipping-container village of micro-restaurants on a former car park
facts: Opened summer 2015 on a disused council car park off Brixton Station Road
Built from upcycled shipping containers by Turner Works for Lambeth Council
Granted a three-year lifespan but became permanent
Houses around ten street-food kitchens, bars, studios and a radio station
w: A council car park rose into a stack of painted shipping containers in 2015, meant to last three years. It refused. Ten kitchens, three bars, a greenhouse and a pirate-spirited radio station crammed into the steel, smoke drifting over the balcony. Temporary, on paper only.

hook: Grade II listed 1920s arcades, heart of Afro-Caribbean London turned food destination
facts: Market Row built 1928; Granville Arcade (now Brixton Village) 1937
Grade II listed in 2010 partly for their Afro-Caribbean social history
Over 100 independent traders across the covered rows
Saved from demolition by a 2009 campaign, then reborn as a dining hub
w: Covered arcades from 1928 and 1937, where Windrush-era Brixton found its yams, salt fish and barbers. Threatened with demolition, then listed in 2010 for exactly that social history. Now a hundred traders trade curries and natural wine under the same concrete trusses. Heritage you can eat.

hook: Compact weekend yard of street-food kitchens and makers behind London Fields
facts: Sits on Westgate Street beside Broadway Market, opened 2011
Run by Eat Work Art on a former bus-and-cab depot site
Mix of permanent food units, coffee stands and traders
Open weekends, with adjoining studios and the Netil360 rooftop
w: Tucked behind Broadway Market on an old cab-and-bus yard, this scruffy weekend pocket trades in tacos, dumplings and flat whites from a huddle of sheds and containers. Makers sell alongside the cooks; a rooftop bar perches above. Small, sharp-elbowed, gloriously East London.

hook: Lunchtime street-food market on one of London's oldest market streets
facts: Trading since at least the early 1800s, possibly the 17th century
Once nicknamed 'Squalors' Market' in its Victorian poverty
Now up to 50 weekday lunch stalls feeding City and Old Street workers
Runs roughly Monday to Friday, late morning to mid-afternoon
w: Costermongers worked this street under Victoria, and possibly back in the 1600s; the Victorians called it 'Squalors' Market' for its poverty. The grime has gone, the trade hasn't. Fifty weekday stalls now fling Kurdish wraps and bibimbap at the lunchtime office crowd. Old bones, new queues.

hook: A derelict 1950s indoor market reborn as a neon night-time street-food haunt
facts: Set in Lewisham's disused 1950s covered market off the High Street
Run as a seasonal Street Feast venue 2014-2021 before that operator collapsed
Returning summer 2026 under brewer German Kraft
Retro shopfronts, string lights and DJ sets in the old units
w: A 1950s covered market that sat shuttered for years, its old shopfronts strung with bare bulbs and reborn after dark as a street-food warren. Street Feast ran it, then folded; brewer German Kraft revives the whole gritty-glamorous thing in summer 2026. Lewisham's dressed-up ruin, back open.

hook: Street-food courtyard threaded through seven Victorian railway arches
facts: Opened 2016 across and around seven railway arches off Southwark Bridge Road
Sits on the old Bankside goods-yard land near Borough
Includes an on-site brewery and the Omeara music venue
Open-air yard with rotating traders and big-screen sport
w: Seven brick railway arches near Borough, stitched into one rowdy courtyard of souvlaki, fried chicken and Neapolitan pizza, trains rumbling overhead. A taproom brews on site and the Omeara stage hides round the back. Bankside's old goods yard, now fed and watered.

hook: Britain's first purpose-built pedestrian shopping precinct, under a Festival of Britain clock tower
facts: Rebuilt for the 1951 Festival of Britain's Live Architecture Exhibition
Designed by Frederick Gibberd as the heart of the Lansbury Estate
First purpose-built pedestrian shopping area in the UK
Grade II listed clock tower, completed 1952; market regenerated through 2026
w: Visitors paid to tour this as the future in 1951: Frederick Gibberd's Lansbury Estate, the first purpose-built pedestrian precinct in Britain, shown off at the Festival of Britain. Stalls still cluster under his Grade II clock tower in Poplar. Tomorrow's world, gone to lunch.

hook: Victorian Westminster street market that once supplied Parliament's flowers
facts: Started around 1862 by traders moving from nearby Broadway
Between the wars it furnished flowers for Houses of Parliament events
Once served a poor district with fresh food and secondhand clothes
Now a weekday lunchtime run of hot-food stalls for Westminster workers
w: Traders set up here around 1862, and between the wars the stalls cut flowers for grand do's at the Houses of Parliament up the road. The bouquets gave way to bibimbap and Korean rice boxes, sold to civil servants between noon and two. Peak chaos at one o'clock sharp.

hook: 150-year-old East End market that once defied attempts to shut it down
facts: Trading from 1843 when it was technically illegal, legalised 1888
Runs along the line of the buried Roman road to Colchester
Woven into Suffragette history and the roots of grime music
Now trades Tuesdays, Thursdays and Saturdays
w: Costermongers started trading here in 1843 when the market broke the law, and the stalls outlasted every effort to close them, legal at last by 1888. The Roman road to Colchester runs buried beneath. Sylvia Pankhurst rallied here; grime later found its feet. East End grit, three days a week.

hook: Bombed, rebuilt East End market where Sainsbury opened his first shop
facts: John James Sainsbury opened his first shop at 68 Watney Street in 1881
By 1902 over 100 shops and 100 stalls crowded the street
Heavily bombed in the Blitz, then rebuilt and pedestrianised
Trades Monday to Saturday with food, clothing and household goods
w: At number 68, in 1881, a young grocer named John James Sainsbury opened the first of his shops. By 1902 over a hundred stalls jammed Watney Street; the Blitz then flattened much of it. Rebuilt and pedestrianised, it still feeds Shadwell six days a week. Where a supermarket empire was born.

hook: Covered market trading under a 1700 royal charter, now packed with food stalls
facts: A royal charter regulating the market dates to December 1700
Present covered market built 1831, re-roofed in glass and steel by 1908
Once sold meat, fish and dairy with slaughterhouses on its edges
Now one of London's busiest street-food markets within a World Heritage Site
w: A royal charter regulated this market back in 1700; by 1831 it traded meat and fish under a roof, slaughterhouses ringing the edges. The blood and bone are long gone. Now Thai curries, dumplings and craft stalls fill the glass-roofed hall in the heart of maritime Greenwich. Chartered, covered, ravenous.

hook: Street-food and flea-market yard with a graffiti tube carriage on the roof
facts: Open-air venue off St Thomas Street, minutes from London Bridge
Weekend 'Flea London' market sells vintage, vinyl and antiques
Decorated with art installations including a tube carriage and dinosaur
Traders span burgers, croquettes, wood-fired pizza and more
w: An old yard by London Bridge, named for the vinegar trade that once soured the air, dressed up with junk-art: a graffiti-bombed tube carriage perched overhead, a stray dinosaur. Burgers and croquettes by night, a vintage flea market by weekend day. Scruffy, theatrical, very Bermondsey-adjacent.

hook: Saturday farmers-and-street-food market in a Lewisham college car park
facts: Held every Saturday in the Lewisham College car park on Lewisham Way
Founded 2011, focused on local producers and seasonal food
Known for traders like Mother Flipper burgers and Mons cheese
Mixes farmers, fishmongers, bakers and gourmet street food
w: Every Saturday a college car park on Lewisham Way fills with farmers, fishmongers and a cult burger stall called Mother Flipper, the queue snaking past wheels of Alpine cheese. No frills, no roof, just seasonal produce and griddle smoke since 2011. South London does its weekly shop, then eats it.
